title: Climate Feedback Loops
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive causal loop diagram showing how positive feedback loops in the climate system amplify global warming through ice-albedo, permafrost methane, and water vapor mechanisms

learningObjectives: Trace how positive feedback loops in the climate system amplify warming
Diagram the causal chain in the ice-albedo, permafrost-methane, and water vapor feedback loops
Distinguish between positive and negative feedback in Earth systems
prerequisites: Basic understanding of the greenhouse effect
Familiarity with the concept of albedo

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Systems thinking through interactive causal loop diagrams
keyQuestions: How does the ice-albedo feedback loop amplify Arctic warming?
Why is permafrost thawing considered a potential climate tipping point?
What is the difference between a positive and negative feedback loop?
How do multiple feedback loops interact to make climate projections more uncertain?
Why is water vapor considered a feedback rather than a climate forcing?
commonMisconceptions: Students often confuse positive feedback with a good outcome -- in systems science, positive feedback means amplifying, which can amplify harmful changes
Many students think climate change is a simple linear process rather than a complex system with reinforcing feedback loops
Students may believe that stopping emissions immediately would halt all warming, not accounting for committed warming from feedback loops already in motion
assessmentOpportunities: Students trace each feedback loop as a written causal chain sequence
Students draw their own causal loop diagram for a negative feedback in the climate system
Students explain why the Arctic is warming faster than the global average using feedback concepts
